title: Left view of tree
description: Print the nodes visible when the binary tree is viewed from the left side

examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize result = [], queue = [1]. Level 0: queue size = 1, first node = 1 → add 1 to result. Process node 1: enqueue children 2 and 3 → queue = [2,3]. Level 1: queue size = 2, first node = 2 → add 2 to result. Process node 2: enqueue child 5 (left child null) → queue = [3,5]. Process node 3: enqueue child 4 (left child null) → queue = [5,4]. Level 2: queue size = 2, first node = 5 → add 5 to result. Process node 5: no children → queue = [4]. Process node 4: no children → queue = []. Level 3: queue size = 1, first node = 4 → add 4 to result. Final result = [1,2,5,4].

Step 1: Initialize result = [], queue = [1]. Level 0: queue size = 1, first node = 1 → add 1 to result. Process node 1: enqueue children 2 and 3 → queue = [2,3]. Level 1: queue size = 2, first node = 2 → add 2 to result. Process node 2: enqueue children 4 and 5 → queue = [3,4,5]. Process node 3: enqueue children 6 and 7 → queue = [4,5,6,7]. Level 2: queue size = 4, first node = 4 → add 4 to result. Process all nodes: no more children. Final result = [1,2,4].

approaches.name: Approach 1: Level Order Traversal with Queue
approaches.summary: Perform a breadth-first search (BFS) using a queue to traverse the tree level by level, and for each level, print the first node encountered. This ensures we capture the leftmost node at each depth.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree

ladders.title: Full Problem — Left View of Binary Tree using Level Order Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print leftmost nodes at each level using BFS with queue
ladders.explanation: The left view of a binary tree consists of the first node visible at each level when viewed from the left side. This problem requires capturing the leftmost node at every depth. The intuition behind using level order traversal (BFS) is that it processes nodes level by level from left to right, making it natural to identify the first node encountered at each level. By performing a standard BFS using a queue, we can track when we reach a new level and record the first node we see at that level. The algorithm works by initializing an empty result list and a queue containing the root node. We then process nodes level by level: for each level, we determine the number of nodes at that level (queue size), iterate through them, and for the first node in that iteration (index 0), we add it to our result. All nodes' children are enqueued for processing in the next level. This ensures we capture exactly one node per level - the leftmost one. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the number of nodes, as we visit each node exactly once. Space complexity is O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree (maximum number of nodes at any level), which corresponds to the maximum queue size. This approach is efficient and intuitive because BFS naturally processes nodes in level order from left to right. Common mistakes include not handling empty trees, incorrectly tracking level boundaries, or adding multiple nodes from the same level. The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete level order traversal algorithm described there.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize result = [], queue = [1]. Level 0: queue size = 1, first node = 1 → add 1 to result. Process node 1: enqueue children 2 and 3 → queue = [2,3]. Level 1: queue size = 2, first node = 2 → add 2 to result. Process node 2: enqueue child 5 (left child null) → queue = [3,5]. Process node 3: enqueue child 4 (left child null) → queue = [5,4]. Level 2: queue size = 2, first node = 5 → add 5 to result. Process node 5: no children → queue = [4]. Process node 4: no children → queue = []. Level 3: queue size = 1, first node = 4 → add 4 to result. Final result = [1,2,5,4].

Step 1: Initialize result = [], queue = [1]. Level 0: queue size = 1, first node = 1 → add 1 to result. Process node 1: enqueue children 2 and 3 → queue = [2,3]. Level 1: queue size = 2, first node = 2 → add 2 to result. Process node 2: enqueue children 4 and 5 → queue = [3,4,5]. Process node 3: enqueue children 6 and 7 → queue = [4,5,6,7]. Level 2: queue size = 4, first node = 4 → add 4 to result. Process all nodes: no more children. Final result = [1,2,4].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Level Order Traversal (BFS) Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement BFS to traverse a binary tree level by level
ladders.explanation: Level order traversal, also known as Breadth-First Search (BFS), is a fundamental tree traversal technique that visits nodes level by level from top to bottom and left to right. The intuition is to process all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next depth, which naturally aligns with how we view trees horizontally. This is achieved using a queue data structure that follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) ordering. The algorithm starts by enqueuing the root node. Then, while the queue is not empty, we dequeue a node, process it (e.g., print or store its value), and enqueue its left and right children (if they exist). This ensures that nodes are processed in the order they were discovered, maintaining level order. The time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(w) where w is the maximum width of the tree, representing the maximum number of nodes at any level stored in the queue. This sub-routine is crucial for the left view problem because it provides the level-by-level processing needed to identify the first node at each level. Common mistakes include forgetting to check if children exist before enqueuing, which can lead to null pointer errors, or using a stack instead of a queue, which would result in depth-first traversal. The connection to L0 is that this BFS implementation forms the core traversal mechanism for the complete left view solution.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue = [1]. Dequeue 1 → process 1, enqueue children 2 and 3 → queue = [2,3]. Dequeue 2 → process 2, enqueue no children (both null) → queue = [3]. Dequeue 3 → process 3, enqueue no children → queue = []. Result = [1,2,3].

Step 1: queue = [1]. Dequeue 1 → process 1, enqueue 2 and 3 → queue = [2,3]. Dequeue 2 → process 2, enqueue 4 and 5 → queue = [3,4,5]. Dequeue 3 → process 3, enqueue no children → queue = [4,5]. Dequeue 4 → process 4 → queue = [5]. Dequeue 5 → process 5 → queue = []. Result = [1,2,3,4,5].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Identifying First Node at Each Level
ladders.desc: Algorithm to capture only the first node encountered during level traversal
ladders.explanation: The core insight for the left view problem is recognizing that we need only the first node at each level during a level order traversal. While BFS processes all nodes level by level, we must modify it to extract just the initial node from each level. The intuition is that as we traverse each level from left to right, the first node we encounter is the leftmost node at that depth, which is exactly what appears in the left view. The algorithm enhancement involves tracking level boundaries. During BFS, we process nodes in batches corresponding to each level. We can do this by noting the queue size at the start of processing a level (which equals the number of nodes at that level). Then, as we iterate through these nodes, we record only the first one (at index 0 of the current level's nodes). This requires adding logic to identify when we start a new level and capturing the appropriate node. Time complexity remains O(n) since we still visit each node once. Space complexity is O(w) for the queue. This logic is essential because it transforms a complete level order traversal into one that extracts specific nodes. Common mistakes include recording every node instead of just the first, or incorrectly resetting level tracking. The connection to L1 is that this builds upon the basic BFS implementation by adding the level-based node selection.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start level with queue containing [2,3]. Queue size = 2 indicates 2 nodes at this level. Iterate i from 0 to 1: at i=0, node=2 → record 2 as first node. Continue to i=1, node=3 → ignore. Result = [2].
Step 1: Queue contains [4,5,6,7] at start of level. Size = 4. Iterate i from 0 to 3: at i=0, node=4 → record 4. Nodes 5,6,7 are ignored. Result = [4].

ladders.title: Building Block — Queue Operations for Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Using queue FIFO operations to manage nodes during BFS
ladders.explanation: A queue is a linear data structure that follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle, meaning the first element added is the first one removed. In tree traversal, queues are essential for BFS because they ensure nodes are processed in the order they are discovered, which corresponds to level order. The intuition is that as we discover nodes at the current level, we enqueue them, and when we process nodes, we dequeue them in the same order, naturally moving level by level. Key operations include enqueue (add to rear) and dequeue (remove from front). For tree traversal, we start by enqueuing the root. Then, while the queue isn't empty, we dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue its children. This maintains the level order because children of earlier nodes are enqueued before children of later nodes at the same level. Time complexity for each enqueue/dequeue is O(1), so overall traversal is O(n). Space complexity is O(w) for storing nodes in the queue. This building block is fundamental because without proper queue usage, BFS wouldn't work correctly. Common mistakes include using a stack (LIFO) instead, which leads to DFS, or not checking for empty queues before dequeuing. The connection to L2 is that queues enable the level-by-level processing needed to identify first nodes.

examples.input: Initial queue: [1], process node 1 with children 2 and 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = [1]. Dequeue → get 1, process it. Enqueue children 2 and 3: add 2 to rear → queue = [2], add 3 to rear → queue = [2,3]. Final queue = [2,3].

examples.input: Queue: [2,3], process node 2 with children 4 and 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue = [2,3]. Dequeue → get 2, process it. Enqueue children 4 and 5: add 4 → queue = [3,4], add 5 → queue = [3,4,5]. Final queue = [3,4,5].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Node Access and Child Navigation
ladders.desc: Accessing a binary tree node's value and its left/right children
ladders.explanation: Binary trees are composed of nodes, where each node contains a value and references to its left and right children. The basic operation involves accessing a node's data and navigating to its children. In most implementations, a node has attributes: value (or data), left (pointer to left child), and right (pointer to right child). The intuition is that trees are recursive structures; starting from the root, we can traverse by moving to children. To access a node's value, we use node.value. To check or move to children, we use node.left and node.right, which may be null if the child doesn't exist. This operation is fundamental because all tree algorithms rely on accessing node data and traversing edges. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity is O(1) per access. Common mistakes include not checking for null children before accessing, which causes null pointer errors, or confusing left and right children. The connection to L3 is that queue operations depend on correctly accessing nodes to enqueue their children.

examples.input: Node with value 1, left child 2, right child 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node object. Access node.value → 1. Access node.left → returns node with value 2. Access node.right → returns node with value 3. If children are null, they would return null.

examples.input: Node with value 2, left child null, right child 5
examples.trace: Step 1: Access node.value → 2. Access node.left → null (no left child). Access node.right → node with value 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Structure and Terminology
ladders.desc: Understanding what a binary tree is: nodes, root, children, levels
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure consisting of nodes, where each node has at most two children: left and right. It starts with a root node at the top, and nodes are connected by edges. Key terminology includes: root (top node), parent (node with children), child (node connected to a parent), leaf (node with no children), level (depth from root, with root at level 0), and subtree (a tree formed by a node and its descendants). The intuition is that trees organize data in a branching structure, unlike linear structures like arrays. They are used to represent hierarchical relationships. For example, a binary tree can represent expressions or file systems. This foundation is crucial because all tree algorithms assume this structure. There's no specific algorithm here; it's about understanding the blueprint. Common mistakes include confusing binary trees with binary search trees (which have ordering) or misunderstanding level numbering. The connection to L4 is that node access operations rely on this structural understanding.

examples.input: Tree with root 1, left child 2, right child 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify root node with value 1. Level 0 contains just root: [1]. Children of root: left=2, right=3, so level 1 contains [2,3]. Both 2 and 3 have no children, so they are leaves.

examples.input: Tree with root 4, left child 5, right child null
examples.trace: Step 1: Root is 4. Level 0: [4]. Children: left=5, right=null, so level 1: [5]. Node 5 has no children, so it's a leaf.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Depth-First Recursive with Level Tracking
approaches.summary: Use a recursive depth-first search (DFS) to traverse the tree, keeping track of the current depth and maintaining a list or variable to store the first node seen at each depth. This leverages the order of traversal to identify leftmost nodes.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) for recursion stack, where h is the height of the tree

ladders.title: Full Problem — Left View of Binary Tree using DFS with Level Tracking
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print leftmost nodes at each depth using recursive DFS with level tracking.
ladders.explanation: This solution implements the left view of a binary tree using a depth-first recursive approach with level tracking. The intuition is that during a pre-order traversal (root-left-right), the first node encountered at each depth is the leftmost visible node from that level when viewed from the left side. We maintain a list 'result' to store the first node seen at each depth, and a 'current_level' parameter to track depth during recursion. The algorithm starts at the root with level 0. At each node, if the current level equals the length of the result list (meaning we haven't seen a node at this depth yet), we add the node's value to result. Then we recursively traverse the left subtree first (crucial for left view), then the right subtree, incrementing the level by 1 for each recursive call. The time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node exactly once. Space complexity is O(h) for the recursion stack, where h is the tree height, plus O(h) for the result list. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete recursive traversal with the level-checking logic. Common mistakes include: 1) Not checking if result size equals current level before adding (would add multiple nodes per level), 2) Traversing right before left (would give right view instead), 3) Not handling null/empty trees properly. The real pattern: def left_view_dfs(root, result, level): if not root: return; if level == len(result): result.append(root.val); left_view_dfs(root.left, result, level+1); left_view_dfs(root.right, result, level+1).

examples.trace: Start: root=1, result=[], level=0. Level 0: result size=0, add 1 → result=[1]. Recurse left: node=2, level=1. Level 1: result size=1, add 2 → result=[1,2]. Recurse left: node=4, level=2. Level 2: result size=2, add 4 → result=[1,2,4]. Recurse left: null, return. Recurse right: null, return. Back to node=2: recurse right: node=5, level=2. Level 2: result size=3 (already has 4), skip. Recurse left/right: null, return. Back to root=1: recurse right: node=3, level=1. Level 1: result size=2 (already has 2), skip. Recurse left: node=6, level=2. Level 2: result size=3 (already has 4), skip. Recurse right: node=7, level=2: skip. Final result=[1,2,4].

Start: root=1, result=[], level=0. Level 0: add 1 → [1]. Recurse left: node=2, level=1. Level 1: add 2 → [1,2]. Recurse left: null. Recurse right: node=4, level=2. Level 2: add 4 → [1,2,4]. Recurse left: node=6, level=3. Level 3: add 6 → [1,2,4,6]. Recurse left/right: null. Back to node=4: recurse right: null. Back to root=1: recurse right: node=3, level=1. Level 1: skip (has 2). Recurse left: node=5, level=2. Level 2: skip (has 4). Final result=[1,2,4,6].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS Traversal with Level Parameter
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive DFS function that traverses tree with level tracking.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core recursive function for DFS traversal with level tracking. The intuition is that we need to traverse the entire tree while maintaining depth information to identify when we first reach each level. The function takes three parameters: the current node, a result list (passed by reference), and the current depth level. The base case is when node is null - we simply return. For non-null nodes, we check if the current level equals the length of result list. This check is crucial because result length indicates how many levels we've already captured - if level equals length, it means this is the first time we're visiting this depth, so we should record this node. Then we recursively call the function on the left child first (important for left view), then on the right child, incrementing level by 1 for each recursive call. The order left-then-right ensures we encounter leftmost nodes first at each depth. Time complexity is O(n) as we visit all nodes. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. This connects to L0 by providing the complete traversal logic that L0 builds upon with the specific left-view condition. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to pass result by reference (should be mutable list), 2) Not incrementing level correctly in recursive calls, 3) Wrong traversal order (right before left). Real pattern: def dfs(node, result, level): if not node: return; if level == len(result): # condition for recording; dfs(node.left, result, level+1); dfs(node.right, result, level+1).

examples.trace: Call dfs(1, [], 0): node=1, result=[], level=0. Check: level=0, result length=0 → condition true (would record if implementing full solution). Recurse left: dfs(2, [], 1). Node=2, result=[], level=1. Check: level=1, length=0 → condition true. Recurse left: dfs(null, [], 2) → return. Recurse right: dfs(null, [], 2) → return. Back to node=1: recurse right: dfs(3, [], 1). Node=3, result=[], level=1. Check: level=1, length=0 → condition true. Recurse left/right: null calls. Traversal completes visiting all nodes with correct level tracking.

Call dfs(1, [], 0): node=1, result=[], level=0. Check: condition true. Recurse left: dfs(null, [], 1) → return. Recurse right: dfs(2, [], 1). Node=2, result=[], level=1. Check: condition true. Recurse left: dfs(null, [], 2) → return. Recurse right: dfs(null, [], 2) → return. Complete traversal with level values: node 1 at level 0, node 2 at level 1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — First Node Capture at Each Depth During Pre-order Traversal
ladders.desc: The insight that first node encountered at each depth during pre-order traversal is the leftmost visible node.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the fundamental insight behind the DFS left view approach: during a pre-order traversal (root, left subtree, right subtree), the first node we encounter at each depth is guaranteed to be the leftmost node visible from that level when viewing from the left side. The intuition works because: 1) Pre-order traversal visits the root first, then recursively traverses left subtree before right subtree. 2) When we go down a level, we always explore the leftmost path first due to the left-first recursion order. 3) Therefore, the first time we reach any particular depth during traversal, we're guaranteed to be on the leftmost node at that depth. The algorithm maintains a result list and checks at each node: if current depth level equals the length of result list, this means we haven't recorded any node for this depth yet, so we add current node's value. Since we traverse left before right, the first node added for each depth will be the leftmost one. Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per node check. Space complexity is O(h) for tracking result. This connects to L1 by providing the condition that gets implemented in the recursive function. Common mistakes: 1) Misunderstanding why left-first traversal matters (right-first would give right view), 2) Not realizing result length naturally tracks captured levels, 3) Trying to use post-order or in-order traversal (won't work). Real pattern: if level == len(result): result.append(node.val).

examples.output: First nodes at each depth: level 0: 1, level 1: 2
examples.trace: Process node 1 at level 0: result=[], length=0, level=0 → equal, record 1 → result=[1]. Process node 2 at level 1: result=[1], length=1, level=1 → equal, record 2 → result=[1,2]. Process node 3 at level 1: result=[1,2], length=2, level=1 → not equal (2≠1), skip. Final first nodes: level 0: 1, level 1: 2. This matches left view.

examples.output: First nodes at each depth: level 0: 1, level 1: 2, level 2: 3
examples.trace: Node 1 level 0: result=[], length=0, level=0 → record 1 → [1]. Node 2 level 1: result=[1], length=1, level=1 → record 2 → [1,2]. Node 3 level 2: result=[1,2], length=2, level=2 → record 3 → [1,2,3]. All nodes are first at their depths since tree is right-skewed.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tree Depth Tracking During Recursive Traversal
ladders.desc: Maintain and propagate depth level information through recursive calls.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on tracking depth levels during recursive tree traversal. The intuition is that as we recursively descend into a tree, we need to know our current depth to identify which level each node belongs to. We achieve this by passing a 'level' parameter that increments by 1 each time we recurse to a child node. Starting from the root at level 0, when we call recursively on left or right children, we pass 'level+1' to indicate we've gone one level deeper. This creates a simple but powerful mechanism: each recursive call knows exactly how deep it is in the tree. The algorithm: function traverse(node, level): if node is null, return; process node with current level; traverse(node.left, level+1); traverse(node.right, level+1). Time complexity is O(n) for visiting nodes. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. This connects to L2 by providing the level information needed for the first-node capture logic. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to increment level when recursing (would keep same level), 2) Using a global variable instead of parameter (would need resetting), 3) Starting level at 1 instead of 0. Real pattern: def traverse(node, level): if not node: return; # use level; traverse(node.left, level+1); traverse(node.right, level+1).

examples.trace: traverse(1, 0): process node 1 at level 0. traverse(2, 1): process node 2 at level 1. traverse(null, 2) → return. traverse(null, 2) → return. Back to node 1: traverse(3, 1): process node 3 at level 1. traverse(null, 2) → return. traverse(null, 2) → return. All nodes processed with correct levels.
traverse(1, 0): process level 0. traverse(null, 1) → return. traverse(2, 1): process level 1. traverse(null, 2) → return. traverse(null, 2) → return. Correct level tracking achieved.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Recursive Tree Traversal (Pre-order)
ladders.desc: Traverse binary tree using recursive pre-order traversal (root, left, right).
ladders.explanation: This basic operation implements recursive pre-order traversal of a binary tree. Pre-order traversal visits nodes in the order: root node first, then recursively traverse left subtree, then recursively traverse right subtree. The intuition is simple: process the current node, then explore its left children fully before exploring right children. This creates a natural top-down, left-prioritized exploration of the tree. The algorithm: function preorder(node): if node is null, return; process node.value; preorder(node.left); preorder(node.right). The base case handles empty trees. Time complexity is O(n) where n is number of nodes. Space complexity is O(h) for recursion stack. This connects to L3 by providing the traversal framework that gets enhanced with level tracking. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting base case (infinite recursion), 2) Wrong order (post-order or in-order), 3) Not handling null children properly. Real pattern: def preorder(node): if not node: return; print(node.val); preorder(node.left); preorder(node.right).

examples.trace: Start at root 1: process 1. Go left to 2: process 2. 2's left: null → return. 2's right: null → return. Back to 1: go right to 3: process 3. 3's left: null → return. 3's right: null → return. Traversal order: 1, 2, 3.
Start at 1: process 1. Left child null → return. Right child 2: process 2. 2's left null → return. 2's right 3: process 3. 3's left/right null → return. Order: 1, 2, 3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Recursion Basics
ladders.desc: Understand binary tree node representation and simple recursive function calls.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers the basic concepts needed: binary tree node structure and recursion. A binary tree node typically has three components: a value (data stored), a left child reference (pointer to left subtree), and a right child reference (pointer to right subtree). In code, this is often represented as a class with attributes val, left, and right. Recursion is a programming technique where a function calls itself to solve smaller instances of the same problem. For trees, recursion works naturally because each subtree is itself a tree. The key elements are: 1) Base case - condition that stops recursion (often when node is null), 2) Recursive case - function calls itself with smaller subproblems (child nodes). Intuition: To process a tree, process the root, then recursively process left and right subtrees. Time complexity depends on number of nodes visited. Space complexity includes recursion stack depth. This connects to L4 as the fundamental building block for tree traversal algorithms. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Not understanding null represents empty tree/subtree, 2) Infinite recursion from missing base case, 3) Confusion about how recursive calls unwind. Real pattern: class Node: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Recursive pattern: def process(node): if node is None: return # base case; # process node; process(node.left); process(node.right).

examples.input: Node with value 5, left child with value 3, right child null
examples.trace: Create root node with val=5. Create left child node with val=3. Set root.left = left child node. Set root.right = None (null). This represents a small tree: root 5 with left child 3 and no right child. For recursion: function print_tree(node): if node is None: return; print(node.val); print_tree(node.left); print_tree(node.right). Call print_tree(root): node=5, print 5; call print_tree(left child): node=3, print 3; call print_tree(None) → return; call print_tree(None) → return; back to root: call print_tree(None) → return.

examples.input: Simple recursive function to count nodes
examples.output: Count = 3 for tree with root 1, left 2, right 3
examples.trace: Define count_nodes(node): if node is None: return 0; return 1 + count_nodes(node.left) + count_nodes(node.right). For tree: root=1, left=2, right=3. Call count_nodes(1): node not null, return 1 + count_nodes(2) + count_nodes(3). count_nodes(2): return 1 + count_nodes(None) + count_nodes(None) = 1+0+0=1. count_nodes(3): similarly returns 1. So count_nodes(1) returns 1+1+1=3. Recursion correctly counts all nodes.

testCases.input: Node with value 5, left child value 3
Node with value 8, right child value 9
Recursive depth: tree with root 1, child 2, child 3
Tree: root A, left B, right C

approaches.name: Approach 3: Iterative DFS with Stack and Depth Map
approaches.summary: Implement an iterative depth-first search using a stack, where each stack element includes the node and its depth, and update a map or array to record the first node at each depth encountered during traversal. This avoids recursion overhead.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) for stack, where h is the height of the tree

ladders.title: Full Problem — Left View of Binary Tree using Iterative DFS with Stack and Depth Map
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print left view nodes using iterative DFS with stack tracking depth and first node per depth.
ladders.explanation: The left view of a binary tree consists of the first node visible at each depth when viewed from the left side. This problem requires identifying the leftmost node at every level. The iterative DFS approach with stack and depth map provides an efficient solution without recursion overhead. The intuition is that during a depth-first traversal, the first node encountered at each depth is the leftmost node at that level when traversing in a specific order (typically root-left-right). By maintaining a stack that stores both node and its depth, we can traverse the tree while keeping track of the first node seen at each depth level. We use a map (or array) to record the first node value encountered for each depth. The algorithm starts by pushing the root node with depth 0 onto the stack. While the stack is not empty, we pop a node and check if its depth hasn't been recorded in our map. If not, we add it as the first node at that depth. Then we push the right child first (if exists) followed by the left child (if exists) onto the stack with depth+1. This order ensures that left children are processed before right children at the same depth, making the first encountered node at each depth the leftmost one. The time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once. Space complexity is O(h) for the stack where h is the tree height, plus O(h) for the depth map. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the depth-first traversal with the depth tracking mechanism. Common mistakes include incorrect stack push order (should push right before left), forgetting to track depth in stack elements, and not handling empty tree cases. Real code pattern: Initialize stack with (root, 0) if root exists, create empty result map, while stack not empty: pop (node, depth), if depth not in map: map[depth]=node.val, if node.right: push (node.right, depth+1), if node.left: push (node.left, depth+1), finally return map values in depth order.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize stack with (node1, depth0). Map={}. Stack=[(1,0)].
Step 2: Pop (1,0). Depth 0 not in map, add map[0]=1. Push right child (3,1) then left child (2,1). Stack=[(3,1),(2,1)].
Step 3: Pop (2,1). Depth 1 not in map, add map[1]=2. Push right child (5,2) then left child (none). Stack=[(3,1),(5,2)].
Step 4: Pop (5,2). Depth 2 not in map, add map[2]=5. Push right child (none) then left child (none). Stack=[(3,1)].
Step 5: Pop (3,1). Depth 1 already in map, skip. Push right child (4,2) then left child (none). Stack=[(4,2)].
Step 6: Pop (4,2). Depth 2 already in map, skip. No children. Stack=[].
Step 7: Map has values at depths 0,1,2: [1,2,5]. But wait, we missed depth 3? Actually tree height is 3 with node4 at depth 2? Let's recalculate: Root1 depth0, node2 depth1, node5 depth2, node3 depth1, node4 depth2. So depths are 0,1,2. Output should be [1,2,5]. But problem says output [1,2,5,4]? Let's check tree structure: 1 has left2 right3, 2 has right5, 3 has right4. Left view: depth0:1, depth1:2 (left child of 1), depth2:5 (leftmost at depth2 is 5, not 4), depth3:none. So output [1,2,5]. The example output [1,2,5,4] is incorrect for this tree. Let's use correct tree: [1,2,3,null,5,null,4] actually gives depths: 1(0),2(1),5(2),3(1),4(2). Left view: depth0:1, depth1:2, depth2:5. So output [1,2,5]. I'll correct the example.
Corrected trace: Using tree [1,2,3,null,5,6,4] where 2 has left5 right6, 3 has right4. Step 1: Stack[(1,0)], Map{}.
Step 2: Pop(1,0), map[0]=1, push(3,1), push(2,1). Stack[(3,1),(2,1)].
Step 3: Pop(2,1), map[1]=2, push(6,2), push(5,2). Stack[(3,1),(6,2),(5,2)].
Step 4: Pop(5,2), map[2]=5, no children. Stack[(3,1),(6,2)].
Step 5: Pop(6,2), depth2 in map, skip. Stack[(3,1)].
Step 6: Pop(3,1), depth1 in map, skip, push(4,2). Stack[(4,2)].
Step 7: Pop(4,2), depth2 in map, skip. Stack[]. Output map values: [1,2,5].

Step 1: Stack[(1,0)], Map{}.
Step 2: Pop(1,0), map[0]=1, push right(2,1), no left. Stack[(2,1)].
Step 3: Pop(2,1), map[1]=2, push right(3,2), no left. Stack[(3,2)].
Step 4: Pop(3,2), map[2]=3, push right(4,3), no left. Stack[(4,3)].
Step 5: Pop(4,3), map[3]=4, no children. Stack[]. Output: [1,2,3,4].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative DFS Traversal with Depth Tracking
ladders.desc: Implement iterative depth-first traversal using stack that tracks node depth.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the core traversal mechanism for the left view problem. The goal is to traverse a binary tree iteratively using a stack while maintaining depth information for each node. The intuition is that DFS can be implemented without recursion by explicitly managing a stack data structure. By storing both the node and its depth together in the stack, we can know how deep each node is during traversal. The algorithm starts by pushing the root node with depth 0 onto the stack if the tree is not empty. Then, while the stack is not empty, we pop a node-depth pair, process the node (in this context, check if it's the first at its depth), and push its children onto the stack with incremented depth. The key insight is the push order: to simulate a depth-first search that visits left before right at each level, we push the right child first, then the left child. This ensures that when we pop from the stack (LIFO), the left child gets processed before the right child at the same depth. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is pushed and popped once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is the tree height, for the stack storage. This sub-routine connects to L0 by providing the traversal framework; L0 adds the logic to record first nodes per depth. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle null nodes, incorrect depth increment, and wrong push order. Real code pattern: stack = [], if root: stack.append((root, 0)), while stack: node, depth = stack.pop(), # process node here, if node.right: stack.append((node.right, depth+1)), if node.left: stack.append((node.left, depth+1)).

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3], traverse and print nodes with depths
examples.output: Node1 depth0, Node2 depth1, Node3 depth1
examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack[(1,0)].
Step 2: Pop (1,0), print 'Node1 depth0'. Push right child (3,1), push left child (2,1). Stack[(3,1),(2,1)].
Step 3: Pop (2,1), print 'Node2 depth1'. No children. Stack[(3,1)].
Step 4: Pop (3,1), print 'Node3 depth1'. No children. Stack[]. Traversal order: 1,2,3.

examples.input: Tree: [1,null,2,3], traverse and print nodes with depths
examples.output: Node1 depth0, Node2 depth1, Node3 depth2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack[(1,0)].
Step 2: Pop (1,0), print 'Node1 depth0'. Push right child (2,1), no left. Stack[(2,1)].
Step 3: Pop (2,1), print 'Node2 depth1'. Push right child (3,2), no left. Stack[(3,2)].
Step 4: Pop (3,2), print 'Node3 depth2'. No children. Stack[]. Traversal order: 1,2,3.

testCases.expected: Node1 depth0, Node2 depth1, Node3 depth1
Node1 depth0, Node2 depth1, Node4 depth2, Node3 depth1
Node1 depth0, Node2 depth1, Node3 depth2
Node1 depth0, Node2 depth1, Node3 depth2
Node1 depth0, Node2 depth1, Node4 depth2, Node3 depth1
Node1 depth0, Node2 depth1, Node5 depth2, Node4 depth2, Node3 depth1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recording First Node per Depth During Traversal
ladders.desc: Track and store the first node encountered at each depth level during tree traversal.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the essential mechanism for identifying left view nodes: recording only the first node encountered at each depth. The intuition is that in a depth-first traversal with specific order (left before right at each level), the first node visited at a given depth will be the leftmost node at that depth. We maintain a data structure (typically a dictionary or array) that maps depth values to node values. During traversal, whenever we visit a node at a certain depth, we check if that depth has already been recorded in our map. If not, this is the first time we're seeing a node at this depth, so we store its value. If the depth already exists in the map, we skip it because we've already recorded the first node at that depth. The key insight is that the traversal order determines which node is 'first' at each depth. By pushing right child before left child onto the stack, we ensure left children are processed before right children at the same depth, making the first encountered node the leftmost one. Time complexity for this logic is O(1) per node check, O(n) total. Space complexity is O(h) for the depth map. This connects to L1 by adding the recording logic to the traversal framework. Common mistakes include using incorrect data structure (list without checking bounds), not initializing properly, and confusing depth with other metrics. Real code pattern: depth_map = {}, if depth not in depth_map: depth_map[depth] = node.val.

examples.input: Traversal order with depths: (A,0), (B,1), (C,1), record first per depth
examples.trace:
Step 1: Process (A,0). depth_map={}. Depth 0 not in map, add depth_map[0]=A.
Step 2: Process (B,1). Depth 1 not in map, add depth_map[1]=B.
Step 3: Process (C,1). Depth 1 already in map, skip.
Final map: {0:A, 1:B}.

examples.input: Traversal order with depths: (1,0), (3,1), (2,1), (4,2), record first per depth
examples.trace:
Step 1: Process (1,0). depth_map={}. Depth0 not in map, add depth_map[0]=1.
Step 2: Process (3,1). Depth1 not in map, add depth_map[1]=3.
Step 3: Process (2,1). Depth1 in map, skip.
Step 4: Process (4,2). Depth2 not in map, add depth_map[2]=4.
Final map: {0:1, 1:3, 2:4}.

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack-based Iterative Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Traverse a binary tree using an explicit stack without recursion.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to perform depth-first traversal of a binary tree using an iterative stack approach instead of recursion. The intuition is that recursion implicitly uses the call stack, so we can mimic this behavior with an explicit stack data structure. For DFS, we use a Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) stack to manage nodes to visit. The basic algorithm starts by pushing the root node onto the stack if it exists. Then, while the stack is not empty, we pop a node, process it, and push its children onto the stack. The order of pushing children determines the traversal order: pushing right child then left child results in a pre-order traversal (root, left, right) because the left child will be popped first. This is crucial for the left view problem where we need left children before right children at each depth. The key insight is that stacks reverse order: the last item pushed is the first popped. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is visited once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is the tree height, which is the maximum stack size. This connects to L2 by providing the traversal mechanism; L2 adds depth tracking. Common mistakes include infinite loops from not handling null nodes, incorrect push order, and stack overflow in deep trees (though less likely than recursion). Real code pattern: stack = [], if root: stack.append(root), while stack: node = stack.pop(), # process node, if node.right: stack.append(node.right), if node.left: stack.append(node.left).

examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack[1].
Step 2: Pop 1, process 1. Push right child 3, push left child 2. Stack[3,2].
Step 3: Pop 2, process 2. No children. Stack[3].
Step 4: Pop 3, process 3. No children. Stack[]. Traversal order: 1,2,3.

Step 1: Stack[1].
Step 2: Pop 1, process 1. Push right child (none), push left child 2. Stack[2].
Step 3: Pop 2, process 2. Push right child (none), push left child 3. Stack[3].
Step 4: Pop 3, process 3. No children. Stack[]. Traversal order: 1,2,3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Pushing and Popping from a Stack with Node-Depth Pairs
ladders.desc: Manage a stack that stores tuples of tree nodes and their depths.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with a stack data structure that stores pairs (tuples) consisting of a tree node and its depth. The intuition is that we need to keep track of both the node and how deep it is in the tree during traversal. A stack is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure where elements are added (pushed) and removed (popped) from the top. By storing (node, depth) pairs, we can associate depth information with each node as we traverse. The operation starts with initializing an empty stack. To push, we use stack.append((node, depth)) to add a tuple. To pop, we use node, depth = stack.pop() which removes and returns the last added tuple, unpacking it into node and depth variables. The key insight is that this pairing allows us to propagate depth information from parent to children: when pushing children, we use depth+1. This is fundamental for the left view problem where depth determines which level a node belongs to. Time complexity for push/pop is O(1). Space complexity is O(n) for storing all pairs in worst case. This connects to L3 by adding depth tracking to the stack operations. Common mistakes include incorrect tuple unpacking, forgetting to increment depth for children, and pushing null nodes. Real code pattern: stack = [], stack.append((root, 0)), while stack: node, depth = stack.pop().

examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack = [].
Step 2: Push (A,0). Stack = [(A,0)].
Step 3: Push (B,1). Stack = [(A,0), (B,1)].
Step 4: Push (C,1). Stack = [(A,0), (B,1), (C,1)].
Step 5: Pop returns (C,1). Stack = [(A,0), (B,1)].
Step 6: Pop returns (B,1). Stack = [(A,0)].
Step 7: Pop returns (A,0). Stack = [].

examples.input: Push (1,0), pop, push children (2,1) and (3,1), pop both
examples.trace:
Step 1: Stack = [].
Step 2: Push (1,0). Stack = [(1,0)].
Step 3: Pop returns (1,0). Stack = [].
Step 4: Push (2,1). Stack = [(2,1)].
Step 5: Push (3,1). Stack = [(2,1), (3,1)].
Step 6: Pop returns (3,1). Stack = [(2,1)].
Step 7: Pop returns (2,1). Stack = [].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Tree Depth and Levels
ladders.desc: Learn what depth means in a binary tree and how to calculate it.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces the idea of depth (also called level) in a binary tree. Depth is the distance from the root node to a given node, measured in number of edges. The root node has depth 0. Its children have depth 1, their children have depth 2, and so on. Intuitively, depth represents how 'deep' a node is in the tree hierarchy. In the context of the left view problem, we need to identify nodes at each depth level because the left view shows one node per depth. To calculate depth, we start at the root with depth 0. When moving to a child node, we increase depth by 1. For example, if a node has depth d, its left and right children have depth d+1. This is a recursive relationship that we can use during traversal. The key insight is that depth is a property that propagates downward from parent to children. Understanding this is crucial for tracking which level each node belongs to when solving tree problems. Time complexity for depth calculation is O(1) per node if we track it during traversal. Space complexity is O(1) for the depth variable. This connects to L4 by providing the depth value that gets paired with nodes in the stack. Common mistakes include confusing depth with height (which is from node to leaf), starting depth at 1 instead of 0, and not incrementing properly. Real code pattern: depth = 0 for root, for child: depth+1.

examples.input: Tree: root A with left child B, right child C. What are depths?
examples.output: A depth0, B depth1, C depth1
examples.trace:
Step 1: Root A has no parent, so depth = 0.
Step 2: B is left child of A. Depth of B = depth of A + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1.
Step 3: C is right child of A. Depth of C = depth of A + 1 = 0 + 1 = 1.
All depths: A:0, B:1, C:1.

examples.input: Tree: A with left child B, B with left child C. What are depths?
examples.output: A depth0, B depth1, C depth2
examples.trace:
Step 1: Root A depth = 0.
Step 2: B is child of A, depth = 0+1 = 1.
Step 3: C is child of B, depth = 1+1 = 2.
All depths: A:0, B:1, C:2.

testCases.input: Root, left child, right child, left grandchild
Root, left child with two children
Root, right child with left child
